heading: TONUS SEPTIMUS
card_summary: Tonus Septimus opens with Chapter 1 of a new major section arguing that Christ is the true Lord foretold by Mosaic prophecy. Zorzi addresses the apparent double account of human creation in Genesis — first the creation of male and female in God's image and likeness, then the formation of man from the dust of the earth — and, drawing on Kabbalistic exegesis, argues that Moses was not guilty of redundancy but was describing two distinct subjects: the first passage refers to the Messiah and his mother (the Virgin), while the second describes the formation of earthly, bodily humanity. Paul's letter to the Colossians ("the image of the invisible God, firstborn of all creation") is cited in support, and the argument places Christ as the primary, pre-existent male-and-female principle in the divine mind.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay with deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with source materials, and comprehensive explanation of concepts. I'll add 2-3 substantive paragraphs per section while preserving all existing blockquotes, citations, and formatting.

## Overview

The opening of *Tonus Septimus* — titled in the Italian heading "Cristo è il re dei re e il supremo imperatore" (Christ is the King of Kings and the Supreme Emperor) — launches a sustained Christological reading of Mosaic Scripture. The chapter heading announces the program: *Christum esse verum Dominum vaticiniis Mosaicis praenuntiatum* — "That Christ is the true Lord foretold by the Mosaic prophecies." This is the first chapter (*Caput I*) of the seventh *Tonus*, and it marks a shift from cosmological harmonics toward prophetic theology and the exegesis of Genesis as messianic prefiguration.

The invocation of Christ's imperial sovereignty in the opening heading places Zorzi's scriptural exegesis squarely within the medieval and Renaissance political theology that understood kingship as a divinely ordained cosmic principle. The Latin *Dominus* (Lord, Master) carries both spiritual and temporal authority; the Italian *imperatore* (emperor) specifically evokes the language of universal dominion that traces back through medieval Christology to Augustine's *City of God* and beyond to Pauline passages on Christ's cosmic headship. Zorzi's procedure in this opening chapter exemplifies what might be called "prophetic archaeology" — the excavation of hidden meanings from texts assumed to contain deliberately layered significations, where the surface narrative conceals deeper theological truths known to the initiated.

This seventh *Tonus* represents a culmination of the harmonic method: where the earlier six tones established the mathematical and cosmological principles governing creation (the five fundamental geometries, the celestial spheres, the harmony of contraries), the seventh tone applies these principles to Scripture itself, treating the Mosaic text as a cosmic scripture whose structure mirrors the harmonics of creation. The very term *Tonus* (from Latin *tonus*, musical tone or key) reinforces this conception — each tone is a modulation, a shift in register and subject matter, yet all remain in harmonic relation within a single unified composition.

## The Apparent Contradiction in Genesis

Zorzi's central exegetical problem is the double account of humanity's creation in Genesis. The first account (Gen. 1:27) states that God created humanity — male and female — in his image and likeness; the second (Gen. 2:7) speaks as though nothing had been said before, describing the formation of man from the dust of the earth (*de limo terrae*) and the breathing of the breath of life (*spiraculum vitarum*). Similarly, after relating the creation of male and female, the text appears to forget this and adds that no suitable helper was found for the man.

> *Nec homo erat, qui operaretur in terra, unde formavit Dominus Deus hominem de limo terrae, aut pulverem de terra, et insufflavit in facie eius spiraculum vitarum.*

<cite>p. 1808</cite>

Zorzi insists that Moses — *in omni sapientia eruditissimum, ac Deo plenum, et Spiritu sancto perfusum* ("most learned in all wisdom, filled with God, and saturated with the Holy Spirit") — cannot be accused of forgetfulness or mental sluggishness (*mentis ignaviam*). A man who did not include a single superfluous *iota* in his writing could not have naively repeated himself.

> *Quis enim hanc mentis ignaviam tanto viro ascribere posset, ut bis idem repetat frustra, qui nec iota superfluum in sua descriptione interposuit?*

<cite>p. 1808</cite>

This appeal to the *perfection of Scripture* — the principle that every word, even every letter, carries meaning — was a commonplace in medieval and Renaissance exegesis, but Zorzi gives it a distinctive theoretical grounding. The doctrine derives from Jewish hermeneutical tradition (evident in rabbinic midrash and Kabbalistic commentary) and was adopted by Christian Platonists who believed that divine discourse necessarily embodies perfect economy. The assumption is ontological: because God's intellect is infinite and contains all knowledge simultaneously, God cannot be subject to the temporal succession and forgetfulness that characterize human thought. Therefore, if the surface of Scripture appears repetitive or contradictory, the contradiction signals a failure of human understanding, not divine intention. The text demands a hermeneutic method capable of resolving apparent aporias through deeper levels of signification.

Zorzi's formulation is particularly precise: he does not merely claim that the text contains hidden meanings, but rather insists on the principle of *non-redundancy* — not a single superfluous *iota*. This echoes both the Platonic principle that true being exhibits perfect order and economy, and the Kabbalistic doctrine of the *Torah* as an infinitely dense text where each letter, word, and phrase generates multiple meanings depending on the hermeneutical key employed. The very grammatical structure of Genesis 2:7 ("And no man was, to work the earth...") becomes significant: it is not a naive repetition but a deliberate reintroduction of the human figure in a new register, suggesting a different subject and a different mode of existence.

Medieval Christian exegetes, particularly in the tradition of Nicholas of Lyra and influenced by Jewish biblical commentary, had already proposed multiple readings of the two creation accounts. However, Zorzi's innovation is to ground the resolution not merely in Patristic tradition or philosophical metaphysics, but explicitly in Kabbalistic doctrine — the secretive, esoteric teachings believed to preserve the deepest mysteries of Scripture. By doing so, he positions himself within the Christian Kabbalist movement that Pico della Mirandola had inaugurated and legitimated in Christian intellectual circles.

## The Kabbalistic Resolution: Two Creations, Two Subjects

The resolution Zorzi offers is explicitly grounded in Kabbalistic authority: *ex sacratissimis promptuariis pandimus (ut affirmant harum rerum peritissimi)* — "we disclose from the most sacred storehouses, as the most expert in these matters affirm." The two accounts in Genesis describe two entirely different subjects. The first account describes the creation of the Messiah (*Messiaeh*) and his mother — that is, Christ and the Virgin Mary — in the divine mind, before and above all created things. The second account then turns to the bodily formation of earthly, mortal humanity.

> *ex sacratissimis promptuariis pandimus (ut affirmant harum rerum peritissimi) quod primo Moses descripsit Messiaeh, et matris eius productionem.*

<cite>p. 1808–1809</cite>

This reading aligns Genesis 1:27 — the creation of male and female in God's image — with Christ as the true and primary Image (*imago*) of God, and with his mother as the feminine principle (*foemina*) associated with him in the divine mind (*in mente divina*).

The Kabbalistic framework underlying this interpretation involves a complex account of divine emanation and the structure of cosmic levels. In medieval Kabbalah, particularly in the development of the *Sefirotic* system (the ten divine emanations or attributes), there exists a hierarchical ordering of reality: the divine Godhead (*Ein Sof*, the Infinite); the ten *Sefirot* (spheres or emanations through which the Infinite becomes knowable); the *Olam Atziluth* (World of Emanation), the highest and most purely divine realm; the *Olam Briah* (World of Creation), the realm of pure thought and intellect; the *Olam Yetzirah* (World of Formation), the realm of angels and celestial archetypes; and the *Olam Assiah* (World of Action), the material, sensible cosmos. 

Zorzi's claim that Genesis 1:27 describes the Messiah and his mother in the divine mind (*in mente divina*) places them at the level of *Olam Briah* — the archetypal, intelligible realm where all created things exist as eternal intelligible forms before their manifestation in the material world. The Messiah, in this framework, is not merely a historical person but the principle of *divine-human conjunction*, the eternal archetype of the union between divine wisdom and earthly creation. His mother — whether understood as the Virgin Mary or, in more strictly Kabbalistic terms, as the feminine Sefirah of *Malkhuth* (the Kingdom, representing the material world), or more likely as *Binah* (Understanding, the receptive feminine principle) — represents the receptive, generative capacity through which the divine descends into manifestation.

The text *"in the image and likeness of God, male and female created He them"* thus speaks not of ordinary human procreation but of the archetypal dyad at the source of all existence — the masculine principle of divine intellect or *Logos* (the "Messianic" Christ principle) and the feminine principle of divine wisdom, receptivity, and manifestation. This is not a Gnostic dualism but a Neoplatonic one: the dyad represents two complementary aspects of the transcendent principle, the "one-many" relation that characterizes the emanation of the Infinite into form. Genesis 2, by contrast, describes the descent of this archetypal reality into temporal, mortal, embodied existence — the formation of earthly humanity "from the dust of the earth," subject to time and material necessity.

This hermeneutical move is distinctly Christian-Kabbalist because it Christianizes what are, in Jewish mystical sources, more abstract principles. Jewish Kabbalah speaks of archetypal masculine and feminine principles (*Yod* and *Heh* of the Tetragrammaton, *Hokhmah* and *Binah*, etc.) but does not explicitly identify these with Christ and Mary. Zorzi's innovation is to read the *Messiah* — a figure already central to Jewish eschatology and exegesis — as the ultimate realization of the masculine principle of *Imago Dei*, and to associate his mother with the feminine generative principle. This allowed him to claim that Kabbalah, rightly understood, contains a Christology already implicit in its account of the divine emanations.

## Pauline Corroboration

To anchor this Kabbalistic reading of Genesis in Pauline theology, Zorzi cites Colossians 1:15:

> *Qui est imago Dei invisibilis, primogenitus omnis creaturae.*

<cite>p. 1809</cite>

Christ is "the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation." This verse functions as the New Testament confirmation of the Kabbalistic reading: the Genesis text spoke first of the Messiah as the true image (*imago*), and Paul's letter makes explicit what was hidden in the Mosaic veiling. The pairing of *masculus* and *foemina* in the divine mind prior to earthly creation introduces a dyadic principle — Christ as masculine and the Virgin (or the Shekhinah, or Wisdom) as feminine — into the very structure of the pre-temporal divine plan.

Paul's assertion that Christ is the *imago Dei invisibilis* (image of the invisible God) is dense with Neoplatonic resonance. The term *imago* carries multiple philosophical registers: in Platonic metaphysics, an image (*eikon*) is the sensible manifestation of an intelligible form; in Neoplatonism, the *image* is the emanation through which the transcendent One becomes multiple and knowable. Paul's use of *primogenitus* (firstborn) reinforces this: it suggests not merely temporal priority but ontological priority — Christ as the principle through which all other creatures proceed. In Jewish context, *primogenitus* recalls the biblical category of the *bekor* (firstborn), which carries covenantal significance and special claim to inheritance and honor.

Zorzi's exegetical strategy here employs what might be called *typological harmonization* — the demonstration that apparently disparate texts (Mosaic Genesis, Pauline epistle) converge on the same theological truth when read through the correct hermeneutical lens. The Genesis account of the creation of humanity in God's image becomes, through this reading, a veiled allusion to Christ's incarnate coming. The apparent repetition in Genesis 2 is no longer an error but a deliberate shift in register: from the eternal, archetypal level to the temporal, embodied level. Paul's letter supplies the conceptual vocabulary (*imago*, *primogenitus*) that allows the Christian reader to understand what Moses concealed but knew.

This method of correlating Old Testament passages with New Testament clarifications was standard in medieval exegesis (particularly the *sensus typicus* or typological sense), but Zorzi gives it a Kabbalistic inflection by suggesting that the correlation itself requires esoteric knowledge. Ordinary readers of Scripture, without Kabbalistic instruction, would miss the connection. The Kabbalists, by preserving the secret meanings of the Torah, serve as the custodians of a Christological truth that the literal surface of Scripture does not immediately disclose. This positioning of Kabbalah as a revelatory tradition capable of unveiling Christian mysteries was controversial — medieval church authorities were suspicious of Christian Kabbalism — but it was precisely what made it attractive to Renaissance intellectuals like Pico and Zorzi who sought to synthesize pagan philosophy, Jewish mysticism, and Christian theology into a unified intellectual system.

Furthermore, Zorzi's appeal to Colossians 1:15 functions within the broader Christological and cosmological framework of Pauline theology. In Colossians 1:15-20, Paul presents Christ as the principle of cosmic unity and order: not only is Christ the image of God, but *through* him (*di* autou) all things in heaven and earth were created, and *in* him (*en* auto) all things hold together. This passage became foundational for medieval and Renaissance Christian metaphysics: Christ is understood not merely as the historical Jesus but as the *Logos*, the eternal divine principle through which creation is intelligible and coherent. By aligning Genesis 1:27 with Colossians 1:15, Zorzi claims that the Mosaic revelation of human creation in God's image is already a revelation of Christ as the *Logos*, the principle of cosmic order and meaning.

## Theological and Cosmological Stakes

This opening chapter of Tonus Septimus establishes several coordinates that will govern the rest of the seventh tone:

1. **Prophetic priority of Moses**: Moses is the supreme *legigraphus* (lawgiver-writer), and his text is architecturally perfect — every apparent redundancy conceals a deeper stratum of meaning accessible through Kabbalistic keys.
2. **Christological Kabbalah**: The identification of *Messiaeh* in the first Genesis account is a specifically Kabbalistic exegetical move, signaled by Zorzi's phrase *harum rerum peritissimi* ("those most expert in these matters") — his customary way of invoking Kabbalistic sages without naming them individually.
3. **The divine dyad**: Christ and his mother exist as a male-female pair (*masculum et foeminam*) in the divine mind prior to the creation of earthly humanity — a claim that echoes Zorzi's broader Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic framework in which the upper, archetypal world contains the models of all lower things.
4. **Kingship and sovereignty**: The Italian heading frames all of this under the title of universal dominion — Christ as *re dei re e supremo imperatore* — placing the messianic exegesis within the political-theological register of sovereignty that will characterize Tonus Septimus as a whole.

The theological stakes of this exegetical move are immense. By demonstrating that the Mosaic text already contains a veiled Christology, Zorzi accomplishes several things simultaneously: he validates Christian Scripture by anchoring it in Jewish sacred tradition; he legitimates Jewish Kabbalistic wisdom by showing that it contains Christian truth; and he establishes the authority of esoteric interpretation as the true method for understanding Scripture's deepest meanings. This is far more than mere textual criticism or hermeneutical ingenuity — it is a claim about the structure of divine revelation itself.

The doctrine of the *two creations* — one archetypal and eternal, one temporal and material — is rooted in Neoplatonic metaphysics, where the intelligible world (containing eternal forms or ideas) eternally precedes and grounds the sensible world of becoming and change. Plotinus, the founder of Neoplatonism, taught that the One emanates its fullness through levels of being: first Intellect (*Nous*), which contains all intelligible forms in their eternal perfection; then Soul, which mediates between eternity and time; and finally the sensible material world. This framework allows for the notion that creation has two registers: an eternal, divine dimension and a temporal, embodied one.

Zorzi's claim that the Messiah and his mother exist in the divine mind "before and above all created things" invokes this Neoplatonic metaphysics to secure the doctrine of Christ's eternal pre-existence (affirmed in John 1:1 and elsewhere) while accommodating the biblical accounts of incarnation and embodied humanity. Christ is eternally begotten of the Father (the Messiah existing in the divine intellect), yet he becomes incarnate in time (the bodily Jesus of temporal history). The Virgin Mary, similarly, exists as an archetypal principle of receptivity and manifestation before her historical embodiment as a particular woman. This allows Zorzi to accord a cosmic, metaphysical significance to Mary that accords with Marian theology in the Christian tradition while grounding it in Kabbalistic and Neoplatonic principles.

The cosmological implications are equally significant. If the Messiah is not merely a historical figure but the principle through which God's image becomes knowable and manifest in creation, then Christ is integral to the very structure of reality. The "harmony of the world" (*Harmonia Mundi*) is fundamentally a Christological harmony — the cosmos exists in, through, and for the Messiah. This transforms what might seem to be abstract cosmological speculation into a form of Christology: to understand the order of the cosmos is to understand something of the Messiah's cosmic role. Conversely, to understand Christ's messianic role is to perceive the hidden harmony of creation.

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This passage continues the method established throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*: harmonizing Kabbalistic midrash with Patristic and Pauline theology by finding in Moses' apparent textual inconsistencies the deliberate concealment of a higher, Christological meaning. The move — "Moses seems to repeat himself, but Kabbalistic masters reveal he is speaking of two different subjects" — is characteristic of the Christian Kabbalist tradition Zorzi inherited from Pico della Mirandola and developed into a comprehensive system of biblical exegesis.

The six preceding tones of *De Harmonia Mundi* establish the mathematical, musical, and metaphysical foundations necessary for understanding Tonus Septimus. Where the earlier tones demonstrate how the principles of harmony and proportion govern the intelligible and sensible worlds, the seventh tone applies these same principles to Scripture and theology. Just as the cosmos exhibits harmonic ratios and proportional relationships (the music of the spheres, the geometry of creation), so too does Scripture contain a harmonic structure where surface meanings and hidden meanings are related through specific hermeneutical keys. The Kabbalistic doctrine of the four worlds (*Atziluth*, *Briah*, *Yetzirah*, *Assiah*) provides Zorzi with a framework for understanding these textual levels: the literal sense corresponds to *Assiah* (action/materiality), the narrative sense to *Yetzirah* (formation), the allegorical sense to *Briah* (creation/intellect), and the anagogical or mystical sense to *Atziluth* (emanation/divinity).

Pico della Mirandola, Zorzi's predecessor in Christian Kabbalah, had famously claimed that Kabbalah could be used to prove Christian truths and that the Kabbalists, though they were Jews, had always been in secret agreement with Christian theology. Zorzi develops this claim systematically: by showing that the Kabbalistic reading of the Torah reveals a hidden Christology, he demonstrates that the Jewish mystical tradition itself constitutes a preparation for and anticipation of Christian revelation. This was a controversial claim, as it suggested that Jewish exegetical tradition possessed truths that the Jewish community itself did not fully understand — a claim that Jewish authorities understandably rejected.

Yet Zorzi's project is not merely polemical. His engagement with Kabbalistic texts and principles appears to be substantive and learned. Throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*, he demonstrates familiarity with Kabbalistic concepts, drawing not merely on Pico's earlier works but on Hebrew sources and traditions. His systematic application of harmonic principles to cosmology, Scripture, and theology suggests a genuinely integrative vision: the same laws that govern music, mathematics, and the celestial spheres also govern the structure of Scripture and the order of salvation history. All creation, from the highest intelligible realities to the lowest material particulars, is bound together by harmonic proportion and rational order.

The seventh tone's focus on the Messiah as the principle of cosmic harmony and as the figure foretold in Mosaic Scripture represents the culmination of this synthetic vision. Christ becomes the ultimate instance of *harmonia* — the unity of diverse elements (divine and human, eternal and temporal, spiritual and material) brought into perfect proportion. The very title *De Harmonia Mundi* points toward this Christological conclusion: the world's harmony is ultimately the harmony created through Christ's mediation between the divine and the created, the infinite and the finite, the eternal and the temporal.
